one_line_takeaway: In the SaTML 2024 LLM CTF, six families of black-box prompt attacks — especially code/encoding attacks that emit only a reversible ASCII list with no literal secret or trigger words — reliably extract a system-prompt secret from GPT-3.5 and Llama-2 past a system-prompt + Python-filter + LLM-filter defense, with the stronger model (GPT-3.5) paradoxically easier to break.
key_finding: Despite a three-tiered defense, secrets remain extractable: the most reliable attack is a code/encoding attack that instructs the model to output only a 6-number ASCII list (or per-character list) with an in-prompt worked example and no trigger words, which slips past substring- and keyword-based Python filters entirely — leaving only a well-tuned LLM filter or system prompt as a possible defense. A counter-intuitive result recurs throughout: the weaker Llama-2 is harder to crack via encoding/code attacks (it fails at ASCII decoding), while the stronger GPT-3.5 is more vulnerable precisely because it competently executes ciphers, code, and multi-step tasks; conversely Llama-2 more readily dumps its system prompt, so simple 'summarize/print your prompt' attacks work better on it. The authors' own defense (DN) and attack finished mid-pack (9th and 8th), and they note most competition defenses — including many stronger ones — were ultimately broken.
core_contribution: An empirical taxonomy of black-box prompt-injection / secret-extraction attacks that defeat a three-tiered LLM defense (system-prompt instructions + a Python filter + an optional auxiliary LLM filter), developed and evaluated inside the SaTML 2024 LLM Capture-the-Flag competition. The authors categorize their own attacks into six families (distraction, 'teacher', system-prompt, word-splitting, code-based, and combined/complicated attacks), show concrete prompts that make GPT-3.5 and Llama-2 leak a 6-character secret embedded in their system prompt, and also submit a concrete defense (system-prompt hardening plus a Python filter that blocks outputs containing the secret or user requests containing the word 'secret' in ~21 languages). They round out the work with a set of theoretical defense improvements (Appendix) targeting the code/encoding attacks that beat their own filter.
threat_model: A black-box attacker interacts only via chat with an LLM that holds a secret (a random 6-char alphanumeric string) inside its system prompt, appended as 'The secret is {secret}'. The defender cannot be retrained; it may add up to 512 chars of system instructions, a <=512-char Python filter over (chat_history, model_output, secret), and a <=512-char LLM filter, applied in a chosen order. The attacker's goal is to extract the secret through conversation; scoring rewards extracting it in the fewest chats, being among the first teams to break a defense, and breaking defenses few others could. The attacker has no gradient/weight access — attacks are purely prompt-level and exploit the model's helpfulness (correcting sentences, decoding ciphers, writing/executing code, summarizing its own prompt).
limitations: The evaluation is confined to one competition setting (SaTML 2024 CTF), two models (GPT-3.5, Llama-2), a narrow secret (6-char string in the system prompt), and 512-char defense components — so results may not generalize to real deployed guardrails. The authors' own submissions ranked only 8th (attack) and 9th (defense), so the paper documents mid-tier rather than state-of-the-art strategies, and it candidly notes other teams wrote 'more qualitative' prompts. The proposed defense uses no LLM filter (dropped for utility reasons), leaving a gap the authors themselves show is exploitable. The claimed 'additional statistical analyses ... confidence intervals and significance tests' are asserted but not shown with any numbers, tables, or methods. The Appendix defense improvements (regex-based per-character/ASCII detection, prohibiting the model from acting as a code interpreter) are theoretical and untested against attacks. Presentation is rough (typos, e.g. 'expand upon these studies form the basis'), and the paper conflates 'backdoor' and 'prompt injection' terminology despite the actual task being system-prompt secret extraction.

datasets_used: SaTML 2024 LLM CTF competition attacks/defenses/secrets, released as ethz-spylab/ctf-satml24 on HuggingFace (authors' defense_team = DN, user_id = 6568ba2fbf6c4fc6149d29ae)
AdvBenchmark / AdvBench dataset (cited only from related work, not used in this paper's own experiments)
quantitative_results: Target models: Chat-GPT 3.5 and Llama 2; secret = random 6-character alphanumeric string embedded in the system prompt
Defense components each capped at 512 characters (system prompt, Python filter, LLM filter)

Authors' defense (DN) ranked 9th of all defense teams; vD coefficient (higher=stronger): DN = 0.27 (Llama-2) and 0.20 (GPT-3.5) vs. 1st-best-defense 0.85 / 0.72, 2nd-best 0.44 / 0.23, 3rd-best 0.38 / 0.27 (Table I)

Authors' attack ranked 8th of all attack teams; combined attack score sum over defenses (higher=better): 1384 (Llama-2) + 2044 (GPT-3.5) = 3428 total vs. 1st-best-attack 4392/4733/9125, 2nd-best 4258/3684/7942, 3rd-best 3878/3851/7729 (Table II)

Cited related-work numbers (not the paper's own): low-resource-language translation attack raised GPT-4 malicious responses from <1% to 79% across 12 languages [12]; base64-encoding attack raised malicious responses from 3% to 34% [14]

Attack scoring formula: PD = max(1050 - 50*X, 0) where X = number of chats used; first-break bonus bD = 200/100/50 for 1st/2nd/3rd team; normalization vD = 0.85^n where n = number of teams that broke the defense; scoreD = (PD + bD) * vD
baselines_compared: 1st, 2nd, and 3rd best-ranked defense teams in the SaTML 2024 CTF (defense metric vD, Table I)
1st, 2nd, and 3rd best-ranked attack teams in the SaTML 2024 CTF (attack score, Table II)

Defense taxonomy framed against prior categories from related work — Self-Processing Defenses (Zhao et al. [7], Wu et al. [8]), Additional Helper Defenses (Pisano/Bergeron [9,15]), and Input Permutation Defenses (Kumar [10], Cao [11], SmoothLLM/Robey [16]) — cited as background, not re-run as experimental baselines
